body.html: Now let us examine the work done by ‘who’, ‘which’ and ‘that’. Remember, these three pronoun actually refer to a proper noun or a particular noun.
body.plain: Now let us examine the work done by ‘who’, ‘which’ and ‘that’. Remember, these three pronoun actually refer to a proper noun or a particular noun.

body.html: These words refer to the noun going before. Thus, they are doing here the work of a Pronoun. They also join two statements. Thus, they are doing the work of a conjunction (or linking word) also. So, they are known as Relative Pronoun in English grammar. Their forms are as follows:

body.plain: Rules for the Use of Relative Pronouns:

body.html: As a general rule ‘who’, ‘whom’ and ‘whose’ are used for persons only.
body.plain: As a general rule ‘who’, ‘whom’ and ‘whose’ are used for persons only.

body.html: The film is about a man whose wife betrays him.

body.html: ‘Which’ is used for things without life and for animals. It is used in the selective or restricted sense.
body.plain: ‘Which’ is used for things without life and for animals. It is used in the selective or restricted sense.

body.html: The moment which is lost is lost for ever.

body.plain: The horse which I recently bought is an Arab.

body.html: In the above sentences, ‘there’ appears to be the subject; but the real subject is the noun that follows the verb.

body.html: If the real subject is singular (a table, a clock, some trouble), the verb is singular (is, was, has).
body.plain: If the real subject is singular (a table, a clock, some trouble), the verb is singular (is, was, has).

body.html: If the real subject is plural (ten desks, pictures, complaints), the verb is also plural (are, were, have).
